1: The Psalter (150 poems spanning roughly David's reign to the post-exilic period) was the hymnbook of the Second Temple — used in liturgy from the restoration (Ezra-Nehemiah) through the NT period. The Dead Sea Scrolls (1QPs, 4QPsa-r, and 11QPsa) preserve the most psalms of any biblical book among the Qumran community, indicating the Psalter's centrality in Jewish worship. The Psalter is organized into five books (Ps 1-41, 42-72, 73-89, 90-106, 107-150) mirroring the five books of Moses, each ending with a doxology. The arrangement suggests a canonical editorial design: the Psalter tells the story of Israel's covenant relationship with YHWH from Torah-delight (Ps 1) to universal praise (Ps 150).
2: Torah meditation was a prescribed daily practice in ancient Israel: Deut 6:7 commanded continuous recitation — when sitting, walking, lying down, and rising. The Hebrew word hagah (meditate) primarily means to mutter or recite aloud; biblical meditation was oral and embodied, not merely silent reflection. The practice of repeating Torah on one's lips throughout the day was the Israelite mechanism for formation of character, not just acquisition of information. Second Temple communities extended this into structured learning houses. 'Day and night' is a merism for 'at all times,' defining the blessed person of v1 not merely by what they avoid but by what they are positively attached to.
3: In the semi-arid Levant, a tree with direct access to a running stream (palge mayim, channels of water) was reliably productive regardless of rainfall variation. Rain-fed agriculture was precarious; an irrigated tree flourished even in drought. Jeremiah uses the same image for the person who trusts YHWH (Jer 17:7-8: 'Blessed is the man who trusts in the LORD … he is like a tree planted by water'). The word shathal (planted) is deliberate: not a self-seeded tree but one intentionally placed in a productive location. Covenant life, the Psalm implies, is cultivated by intention, not achieved by nature.
4: Threshing in ancient Israel was performed on elevated hilltops where wind would separate grain from chaff. The harvested stalks were tossed into the air; the heavy grain fell to the threshing floor while the weightless husks blew away. This agricultural scene was visible to every Israelite at the end of the grain harvest each summer. The same image appears in John the Baptist's proclamation (Matt 3:12: the one coming will 'clear his threshing floor … gather his wheat … but burn the chaff') and in Egyptian and Mesopotamian wisdom literature, where the wicked are also described as insubstantial and unstable.
5: 'The wicked shall not stand in the judgment (mishpat), nor sinners in the congregation (qahal) of the righteous' — mishpat here is the formal covenant assembly where verdicts are rendered, not merely an abstract concept of divine justice. The qahal tsaddiqim echoes the language of the Deuteronomic assembly (Deut 23:1-8: who may 'enter the assembly of the LORD'). To 'stand' in the judgment is to maintain one's ground under formal examination; the wicked cannot hold their position when covenant membership is tested. The assembly is both the present community of worshipers and the eschatological gathering at which final verdicts will be announced.
6: 'The LORD watches over (knows) the way of the righteous' — yada (to know) carries the weight of intimate covenant relationship, not mere cognitive awareness. YHWH's 'knowing' of a person is the language of election: 'You only have I known of all the families of the earth' (Amos 3:2). The way of the wicked 'will perish' (toved, from abad) — the same word used of the Exodus generation who died in the wilderness (Num 17:12) and of any destroyed by divine judgment. The Psalm closes by mapping the two paths onto two trajectories of covenant history: faithfulness-to-flourishing and rebellion-to-destruction.
